Essentially, this involves reconstructing the boundaries of regulatory capabilities without altering the asset's form. Simultaneously, the Bank of Korea is explicitly promoting the development of a CBDC and deposit token system, while maintaining a relatively cautious attitude towards stablecoins. The overall path points towards an on-chain monetary structure centered on the banking system, incorporating financial innovation within a controllable financial framework. The UK, on ​​the other hand, takes a different approach: formally integrating stablecoins into its payment system structure through a unified payment regulatory framework, and attempting to cover future AI-driven payment scenarios. Both point in the same direction: stablecoins are transitioning from "unregulated assets" to "regulated financial infrastructure." Europe: Structural Filling the Gap in the "Money Supply Layer" Compared to the three layers mentioned above, Europe's actions are occurring at a deeper level—the money supply structure. The Qivalis consortium, comprised of 12 major European banks, is advancing its plan to issue a euro-denominated stablecoin, regulated by the Dutch central bank and compliant with the MiCA framework. This development signifies a shift in stablecoin issuers from crypto-native institutions to the traditional banking system. The driving force behind this is not product innovation, but rather a structural imbalance within the on-chain monetary system: dollar-denominated stablecoins have effectively become the global basis for pricing and liquidity, while the non-dollar system has long been relegated to the margins. This structure means that as financial activities gradually migrate to the on-chain environment, if a local currency lacks a corresponding stable value carrier, its role in the pricing and settlement system will be systematically weakened. Therefore, the collective entry of European banks is essentially a reconstruction of the euro's monetary existence within the on-chain financial system. This action is closer to an extension of the monetary system than a single product innovation. The True Structure of Stablecoin Competition By juxtaposing the four layers, a clearer global division of labor emerges: USA: Defining rule boundaries (Rules Layer) Japan: Promoting practical use (Usage Layer) South Korea + UK: Building regulatory visibility (Regulatory Layer) Europe: Competing for the right to supply currency (Supply Layer) This structure demonstrates that stablecoin competition is no longer a single-market issue, but a process of restructuring the financial system across multiple layers. The focus of competition is shifting upwards. The "interest payment dispute" among stablecoins is merely a glimpse into the regulatory framework; the real competition is moving to a deeper level. As the four levels of rules, usage, regulation, and supply gradually separate and evolve independently, stablecoins are no longer just financial products, but rather extensions of different national financial systems into the on-chain world. In this process, the future landscape will no longer be determined by a single policy choice, but rather by who can establish a structural advantage at their own level and expand it into a systemic presence. The ultimate fate of stablecoins does not depend on their definition, but on the moment the payment occurs.

data.title: Confusion or opportunity? The crypto market enters a "repricing moment".
data.description:
Since 2026, the crypto market has been under pressure overall, with Bitcoin and mainstream assets experiencing a synchronized correction and a significant cooling of market sentiment. Behind this period of downturn, is it the end of a cycle or a prelude to structural reshaping? Huobi HTX, in its recently released "2026 Digital Asset Trends White Paper," points out that digital assets are completing their historic establishment as an asset class, and the market-driving logic has shifted entirely from "price cycles" to "structural trends." This assessment provides a key explanatory framework for the current market—behind short-term fluctuations lies a profound reshaping driven by macro liquidity, institutional participation, regulatory evolution, and technological infrastructure upgrades. The white paper further concludes that the core issue in 2026 is no longer "whether digital assets have value," but rather "what proportion should they occupy in global asset allocation." Core assets such as Bitcoin are gradually embedding themselves into the traditional financial system, forming a new allocation structure alongside assets like US Treasury bonds and gold; simultaneously, the rise of stablecoins, RWA, and on-chain yield assets is reshaping the flow of funds within the crypto ecosystem. Against this backdrop, the current market downturn is essentially a phase of growing pains during the transition from a high-volatility growth phase to a mature financial system. This article systematically analyzes the current crypto market downturn based on the macro environment, institutional behavior, market structure, and industry trends. It also incorporates Huobi HTX's research perspective to provide a forward-looking analysis of potential turning points and long-term trends, aiming to offer market participants a more framework for judgment.

I. Current Market Situation

II. Analysis of the Core Reasons for the Market Slump

Bullish View (Tom Lee): The lag effect of liquidity trends after the halving has not yet appeared; institutional structural holdings will erupt when the Fed shifts its stance in the second half of the year; target price: $200,000.

... 4. Structural Collapse of the Altcoin Market • A large number of tokens issued in 2024-2025 are facing a lack of buyers. • Retail investor confidence is continuously suppressed by the lessons learned from FTX, Luna, and other precedents. • The AI+meme narrative is rotating too quickly, leading to user fatigue. • Tiger Research predicts that projects unable to generate sustainable income will exit in batches. 5. Geopolitical and War Risks • Continued tensions in the Middle East (temporary US-Iran ceasefire, the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, the Iran-Israel conflict, the Lebanon-Israel conflict) • Tensions among the US, Israel, and Iran continue to suppress risk assets. • Following the ceasefire announcement by Iran on April 8, 2026, Trump announced on April 17 that the Strait of Hormuz was temporarily opened, causing BTC to rebound sharply to 78,000 in a single day, indicating that geopolitical fears are a significant suppressive factor. III. Core Judgments of Various Institutions/Analysts Bullish Viewpoint Bearish/Cautious Viewpoint src="https://img.jinse.com.cn/7454708_watermarknone.png" title="7454708" alt="D12cspgjnMoNgVrsEWAQ88NikuCUzzyUp9fZUOIH.png">

Neutral/Structural Perspective

IV. Stablecoins: Bright Spots Amidst the Downturn

V. Catalysts for a Rebound (When Will the Slump End?)

VI. Huobi HTX's Viewpoint

6.1 Core Judgment on the Market Slump

Huobi HTX believes that the current slump is not the end, but rather the growing pains of a structural transformation period.

"Digital assets are gradually transitioning from 'price cycle driven' to 'structural trend driven.' Factors such as macro liquidity, regulatory framework, institutional participation, and technological advancements are becoming core variables determining the long-term landscape of the industry. Short-term price fluctuations will persist, but what truly influences the industry's direction is the establishment of the asset class's status, the improvement of infrastructure capabilities, and the reshaping of the global capital structure." 6.2 Structural Interpretation of the Reasons for the Downturn A. Liquidity Rebalancing is Fundamental • The Federal Reserve repeatedly weighed the decline in inflation against the resilience of employment, resulting in fluctuating interest rate cuts. • Weak growth momentum in Europe, progress in interest rate normalization in Japan, and changes in the global arbitrage capital structure. • Digital assets are deeply embedded in the global liquidity framework, and their pricing logic is converging with that of traditional macro assets—this is the fundamental reason for the high correlation between BTC and the S&P 500. B. Institutionalization is a maturation process, not a source of risk. "In 2026, the market will no longer focus on 'whether digital assets have value,' but rather 'what proportion should they occupy in asset allocation.' This shift marks the industry's entry into a mature stage." The increased short-term volatility brought about by the rise in institutional holdings is an inevitable stage in the industry's maturation—the increase in institutional holdings will change the market structure, increase the proportion of long-term funds, and lead to a decrease in overall volatility. C. Stablecoins' counter-trend growth validates the value of infrastructure. "Stablecoins are becoming 'on-chain printing presses' for the dollar system, and changes in their liquidity will become an important leading indicator of market risk appetite and capital flows." Currently, the stablecoin market size exceeds $300 billion, and monthly trading volume surpasses that of the US banking network, proving the enduring trust in crypto infrastructure.

6.3 Huobi HTX 2026 Strategic Keywords

6.4 Huobi HTX Five Core Trend Predictions

Trend 1: BTC's Digital Gold Positioning Solidifies

BTC will exist as a structural allocation asset, with volatility tending to converge, the proportion of long-term holders continuing to increase, and market pricing power further shifting to medium- and long-term capital.

Trend Two: ETH Becomes a Core Carrier of Yield Assets Driven by staking and DeFi protocol revenue, ETH is approaching the status of a "growth-oriented yield asset" in institutional portfolios, serving as a core value capture vehicle for on-chain economic activities. Trend Three: Stablecoin Scale Continues to Reach New Highs Stablecoins will expand from a medium of exchange to cross-border payments and on-chain settlements, becoming the third largest channel for global dollar circulation (after the bank deposit market and the US Treasury market). Trend Four: AI Agents Become the Protagonists of the Next Round of Narratives On-chain automated execution will become the focus of the new round of technological competition. Huobi HTX has taken the lead in deploying AI Skills and AI interactive interfaces, making it one of the first mainstream CEXs to integrate AI with exchange capabilities. Trend 5: A Steady Track Amidst RWA's Slump Real-world asset tokenization is entering an accelerated phase, with RWA's scale expected to exceed $1 trillion by 2030, serving as a key bridge between the crypto market and traditional finance. VII. White Paper Perspective: Evidence and Supplement to Long-Term Trends Furthermore, the "2026 Digital Asset Trends White Paper" provides important supplementation and evidence to the above analysis. In Chapter 8, "Key Judgments for the Next Decade," the white paper provides a more forward-looking and systemic perspective on the current structural changes in the market. Regarding stablecoins, the white paper explicitly states that "stablecoins are profoundly changing the structure of the global payment and financial system," and predicts that "if stablecoins continue their current development trend, their market size is expected to reach trillions of dollars in the next decade, and may become the third largest channel for dollar circulation after the US bank deposit market and the US Treasury bond market." This judgment is highly consistent with the analysis in Chapter 4 of this report, "Stablecoins: A Bright Spot in a Downturn"—the current migration of funds to stablecoins is not simply a risk-averse behavior, but reflects a deeper recognition of the infrastructure function of the crypto market. As market participants (including institutions) increasingly use stablecoins as a store of value and a medium of exchange rather than a short-term hedging tool, stablecoins are actually completing a leap from being an "appendage of the crypto market" to becoming the "third pole of global dollar circulation." Regarding the underlying logic of institutional participation, the white paper argues that "the increased short-term volatility brought about by the rise in institutional holdings is a necessary stage in the industry's maturation—the increase in institutional holdings will change the market structure, increase the proportion of long-term funds, and lead to a decrease in overall volatility." This judgment provides a more macro perspective for understanding the current "double-edged sword" effect of ETFs: the short-term selling pressure brought about by institutionalization is essentially an extension of the risk management logic of traditional financial markets into the crypto world, resulting in an accelerated bursting of the industry bubble rather than a change in the industry's long-term development direction. Regarding trends for the next decade, the white paper proposes that "the combination of AI and blockchain may become one of the most important technological trends in the next decade," and predicts that "as the number of AI Agents continues to increase, they may gradually become important participants in the on-chain economy, and even become the main trading entities in some scenarios." This means that the current rapid rotation of AI+meme narratives may only be the initial stage of the combination of AI and Crypto; as the autonomy of AI Agents on the chain increases, the main structure of the on-chain economy will undergo fundamental changes, and the "traders" at that time may no longer be just human investors. Regarding the long-term direction of the market, the white paper's core judgment is: "The development of digital assets is actually a process from technological experimentation to the restructuring of the financial system. In the past decade, blockchain technology has completed infrastructure construction; and in the next decade, digital assets may gradually evolve from an emerging asset class into an important part of the global financial system." Combined with this report's analysis of the current downturn, this judgment means that the current market adjustment is both a painful process of deflating the industry's bubble and an inevitable stage in the transition of digital assets from "highly volatile innovative assets" to "mature financial infrastructure." Short-term price slumps can never negate long-term structural trends—the "more open, efficient, and globalized on-chain financial network" described in the white paper may be the destination the industry will reach after this downturn.
